$\frac{1}{9}x^{2}+y^{2}=1$

A-4 Let A and B be $2\times2$ matrices with integer entries such that $A,A+B,A+2B,A+3B$ ,and $A+4B$ are all invertible matrices whose inverses have integer entries. Show that $A+5B$ is invertible and that its inverse has integer entries.

with $i_{1}<i_{2}<\cdots<i_{1994}$ ↓. Show that every nonempty interval $(a,b)$ contains a nonempty subinterval $(c,d)$ that does not intersect S.

$e_{i}=0$ or 1 for $1\leq i\leq10.\quad(f_{i}^{0}$ is the identity function and $f_{i}^{1}=f_{i}.)$ Show that ifA is any nonempty finite set
of integers, then at most 512 of the functions in $\mathcal{F}$ map A to itself.

A=\begin{pmatrix}3&2\\ 4&3\end{pmatrix}\quad and\quad I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\ 0&1\end{pmatrix}.
Show that $\lim_{n\to\infty}d_n=\infty$

core_content.markdown:
# The 55th William Lowell PutnamMathematical Competition  Saturday, December 3, 1994

A-1 Suppose that a sequence $a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\ldots$ . satisfies $0<a_{n}\leq$ $a_{2n}+a_{2n+1}$ for all $n\geq1$ . Prove that the series $\textstyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_{n}$ diverges.



A-2 Let A betheareaof theregionin thefirstquadrant bounded by the line $y=\frac{1}{2}x$ :, the x-axis, and the ellipse $\frac{1}{9}x^{2}+y^{2}=1$ . Find the positive number m such that A is equal to theareaof the regionin thefirst quadrant bounded bv the line $y=m x$ ,the $y-\alpha\dot{x}\dot{s}$ , and the ellipse 

$\frac{1}{9}x^{2}+y^{2}=1$ 



A-3 Showthat if the pointsof an isosceles right triangle of side length 1 are each colored with one of four colors,then there must be two points of the same color whch are at least a distance $2-\sqrt{2}$ apart.



A-4 Let A and B be $2\times2$ matrices with integer entries such that $A,A+B,A+2B,A+3B$ ,and $A+4B$ are all invertible matrices whose inverses have integer entries. Show that $A+5B$ is invertible and that its inverse has integer entries.



A-5 Let $\left(r_{n}\right)_{n\geq0}$ be a sequence of positive real numbers such that $\lim_{n\to\infty}r_n=0$ . Let S be the set of numbers reresentable as a sum 



$$r_{i_{1}}+r_{i_{2}}+\cdots+r_{i_{1994}},$$

with $i_{1}<i_{2}<\cdots<i_{1994}$ ↓. Show that every nonempty interval $(a,b)$ contains a nonempty subinterval $(c,d)$ that does not intersect S.



A-6 Let $f_{1},\ldots,f_{10}$ be bijections of the set of integers such that for each integer n, there is some composition $f_{i_{1}}\circ$ )$f_{i_{2}}\circ\cdots\circ f_{i_{m}}$ of these functions (allowing repetitions)which maps 0 to n. Consider the set of 1024 functions 

$$\mathcal{\bar{F}}=\{f_{1}^{e_{1}}\circ f_{2}^{e_{2}}\circ\dots\circ f_{10}^{e_{10}}\},$$

$e_{i}=0$ or 1 for $1\leq i\leq10.\quad(f_{i}^{0}$ is the identity function and $f_{i}^{1}=f_{i}.)$ Show that ifA is any nonempty finite set 

of integers, then at most 512 of the functions in $\mathcal{F}$ map A to itself.



B–1 Find all positive integers n that are within 250 of exactly 15 perfect squares.



B-2 For which real numbers c is there a straight line that intersects the curve 



$$x^{4}+9x^{3}+cx^{2}+9x+4$$

in four distinct points?

B-3 Find the set of all real numbers k with the following property: For any positive,diferentiable function f that satisfies $f^{\prime}\left(x\right)>f\left(x\right)$ for all x, there is some number N such that $f(x)>e^{\hat{k}x}$ for all $x>N$ 



B-4 For $n\geq1,\operatorname{l e t}d_{n}$ be the greatest common divisor of the entries of $A^{n}-I$ ,where 



$$A=\begin{pmatrix}3&2\\ 4&3\end{pmatrix}\quad and\quad I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\ 0&1\end{pmatrix}.$$

Show that $\lim_{n\to\infty}d_n=\infty$ 

B-5 For any real number α, define the function $f_{\alpha}(x)=$ [α]. Let n be a positive integer. Show that there exists an α such that for $1\leq k\leq n$ ,

$$f_{\alpha}^{k}\left(n^{2}\right)=n^{2}-k=f_{\alpha^{k}}\left(n^{2}\right).$$

B-6 For any integer n, set 

$$n_{a}=101a-100\cdot2^{a}.$$

Show that for $0\leq a,b,c,d\leq99,\;n_{a}+n_{b}\equiv n_{c}+n_{d}$ (mod 10100) implies $\left\{a,b\right\}=\left\{c,d\right\}$
